longDescription: The RIBA 2030 Climate Challenge sets performance targets for UK architects and building designers across three metrics: operational energy use intensity (kWh/m²/yr), embodied carbon (kgCO2e/m²), and potable water use (litres/person/day). Targets are provided for 2025 and 2030, with separate benchmarks for domestic and non-domestic buildings. The framework aligns with LETI targets and the UK Net Zero Carbon Buildings Standard. RIBA Plan of Work stages are mapped to recommended assessment points, encouraging early-stage embodied carbon estimation and progressive design optimization.
author: Royal Institute of British Architects (RIBA)

license.name: Free download (targets are open source per RIBA)

scope: Operational energy, embodied carbon, and water use targets for new buildings
limitations: UK-focused targets only
Target framework, not a calculation tool
Limited building type granularity compared to LETI
Retrofit targets less developed than new-build targets
citation: RIBA (2023). 2030 Climate Challenge. https://www.architecture.com

name: SE 2050 + ECOM Calculator
description: Structural Engineers 2050 Commitment and Embodied Carbon in Construction Calculator (ECOM) for benchmarking and reducing structural embodied carbon.
longDescription: The SE 2050 Commitment Program, launched by the Structural Engineering Institute (SEI) of ASCE and the Carbon Leadership Forum, challenges structural engineering firms to achieve net-zero embodied carbon in structural systems by 2050. The accompanying ECOM (Embodied Carbon in Construction) Calculator enables engineers to estimate and benchmark embodied carbon of structural systems using material quantities and regional emission factors. The SE 2050 database collects anonymized project submissions to build an industry benchmark database of structural embodied carbon by building type, structural system, and region.
author: Structural Engineering Institute (SEI) / ASCE
scope: Structural system embodied carbon (foundations, superstructure) — Modules A1–A3
limitations: Structural systems only — does not cover envelope, MEP, or fit-out
North American focus in emission factor database
Benchmark database still growing — limited data for some building types
Simplified calculator — for detailed LCA use One Click LCA or Athena
citation: SEI/ASCE (2024). SE 2050 Commitment Program. https://se2050.org

name: OpenLCA
description: Open-source general-purpose life-cycle assessment software supporting all major LCI databases (ecoinvent, GaBi, ELCD) for product and building LCA.
longDescription: OpenLCA is a free, open-source life-cycle assessment software developed by GreenDelta in Berlin. It supports all major LCI databases (ecoinvent, GaBi/Sphera, ELCD, USDA LCI, Agribalyse, NEEDS) and LCIA methods (ReCiPe, CML, TRACI, EF 3.0/3.1). OpenLCA can be used for building-level LCA by importing construction material datasets and modeling full building life cycles. It features contribution analysis, Monte Carlo uncertainty analysis, regionalized LCIA, and social LCA capabilities. The Collaboration Server enables team-based LCA work. Compatible with ILCD, EcoSpold, and JSON-LD data formats.
author: GreenDelta GmbH (Berlin)
scope: Full life-cycle assessment for any product system, including buildings and construction materials
limitations: General-purpose tool — no building-specific templates or wizards
LCI databases (ecoinvent, GaBi) require separate paid licenses
Steeper learning curve than building-specific LCA tools
No direct BIM integration
citation: GreenDelta (2024). OpenLCA v2.2. https://www.openlca.org

name: TOTEM
description: Free Belgian web tool for whole-building environmental assessment covering embodied and operational impacts across the full life cycle.
longDescription: TOTEM (Tool to Optimise the Total Environmental impact of Materials) is a free web-based tool developed by the three Belgian regions (Brussels, Flanders, Wallonia) in partnership with VITO. It calculates the environmental impact of building materials and assemblies across the full life cycle (Modules A1–D) using Belgian-specific environmental data from the B-EPD database and the MMG methodology (Milieugerelateerde Materiaalprestatie van Gebouwelementen). TOTEM combines embodied impacts with operational energy to provide a total environmental cost in monetary units (EUR/m²), enabling trade-off analysis between insulation, structure, and energy performance.
author: Belgian Regions (Brussels, Flanders, Wallonia) / VITO
scope: Whole-building environmental assessment (embodied + operational) in monetised environmental cost (EUR/m²)
limitations: Belgian material database and construction practices only
Environmental cost monetisation methodology specific to Belgium (MMG)
Limited customisation of material properties
Web-only — no offline or BIM-integrated version
citation: Belgian Regions / VITO (2024). TOTEM. https://www.totem-building.be

name: INIES Database
description: French national EPD database containing environmental and health declarations (FDES and PEP) for construction products, mandatory for French RE2020 compliance.
longDescription: INIES is the French national reference database for environmental and health declarations of construction products and building equipment. It hosts FDES (Fiches de Déclaration Environnementale et Sanitaire) for construction products and PEP (Profils Environnementaux Produits) for building equipment, both compliant with EN 15804 and NF EN 15978. INIES is mandatory for RE2020 (Réglementation Environnementale 2020) compliance calculations in France and is used by French LCA tools including ELODIE and Vizcab. The database contains 3,000+ product declarations covering structural, envelope, interior, and MEP products.
author: Alliance HQE-GBC France / INIES Association
scope: Environmental product declarations (FDES/PEP) for construction products — all life-cycle modules
limitations: French products only — limited international coverage
FDES format differs slightly from standard EN 15804+A2 EPDs
No API for programmatic access
Database search interface in French only
citation: Alliance HQE-GBC France (2024). INIES Database. https://www.inies.fr

name: EPD International Library
description: Global EPD programme operator and library hosting thousands of verified environmental product declarations for construction and other sectors based on ISO 14025.
longDescription: EPD International AB operates The International EPD System, one of the world's largest EPD programme operators based in Stockholm. The EPD library contains thousands of verified Environmental Product Declarations for construction products, building equipment, and other sectors, all developed according to ISO 14025, ISO 14044, and EN 15804+A2. EPDs are third-party verified and cover all life-cycle modules (A1–D). The library is freely searchable and machine-readable via the EPD API (ILCD+EPD format). EPD International also operates the Environdec digital platform for EPD creation and management.
author: EPD International AB (Stockholm)
scope: Verified environmental product declarations (EPDs) for construction products across all life-cycle modules
limitations: EPD availability varies by product category and region
Different PCR versions may affect comparability between EPDs
Registration required for API access
Primarily European product coverage despite global scope
citation: EPD International AB (2024). The International EPD System. https://www.environdec.com

name: IBU.data EPD Database
description: German EPD programme operator (Institut Bauen und Umwelt) hosting 2,500+ verified construction product EPDs compliant with EN 15804+A2.
longDescription: IBU (Institut Bauen und Umwelt e.V.) is a leading German EPD programme operator for construction products. IBU.data is its online portal providing free access to 2,500+ verified Environmental Product Declarations covering concrete, steel, insulation, wood products, facade systems, flooring, paints, HVAC components, and more. All EPDs comply with EN 15804+A2 and ISO 14025, are third-party verified by independent auditors, and include all mandatory life-cycle modules. IBU.data provides machine-readable EPD data in ILCD+EPD format and feeds into the ECO Platform aggregator. IBU EPDs are referenced by DGNB, BNB, and ÖKOBAUDAT.
author: Institut Bauen und Umwelt e.V. (IBU), Germany

license.name: Free public access (registration required for XML downloads)

scope: Verified EPDs for construction products — all life-cycle modules per EN 15804+A2
limitations: Primarily German and European product manufacturers
Some older EPDs still on EN 15804+A1 (transition period)
No integrated calculation tool — data only
Search interface primarily in German
citation: IBU e.V. (2024). IBU.data EPD Database. https://ibu-epd.com

name: ECO Platform Portal
description: European EPD aggregator harmonising verified construction product EPDs from 15+ national programme operators into a single searchable portal.
longDescription: ECO Platform is a European consortium of EPD programme operators that provides a unified portal for accessing harmonised construction product EPDs from 15+ national programmes (IBU, EPD International, BRE, EPD Norge, EPD Italy, EPD Denmark, etc.). The portal aggregates EPDs into a consistent digital format based on EN 15804+A2 and the ILCD+EPD data format. ECO Platform's verification ensures mutual recognition of EPDs across European green building certification schemes. The portal enables cross-border comparison of construction products and feeds into national databases like ÖKOBAUDAT and the EU Level(s) framework.
author: ECO Platform AISBL (Brussels)

license.name: Free portal access; paid API license for commercial integration

scope: Harmonised EPD data from 15+ European programme operators — all life-cycle modules
limitations: Aggregator only — original EPDs hosted by national programme operators
Not all EPDs from member programmes are yet digitised in the portal
Coverage varies significantly by product category
No calculation functionality — reference data only
citation: ECO Platform (2024). ECO Portal. https://www.eco-platform.org

name: tallyCAT
description: Free Revit plugin by Building Transparency for calculating embodied carbon of building assemblies using the EC3 database directly within BIM workflows.
longDescription: tallyCAT (formerly Tally) is a free Revit plugin developed by Building Transparency that enables architects and engineers to calculate embodied carbon of building materials and assemblies directly within their BIM model. It connects to the EC3 (Embodied Carbon in Construction Calculator) database of EPDs and maps Revit material definitions to environmental impact data. tallyCAT calculates GWP (kgCO2e) by element, assembly, and whole building, supporting Modules A1–A3 with optional A4, B, C, and D. Results can be exported for LEED MR credits and whole-building LCA documentation.
author: Building Transparency
scope: Embodied carbon of building materials and assemblies from BIM model (Modules A1–A3, optional A–D)
limitations: Requires Autodesk Revit (Windows only)
Dependent on EC3 database coverage for product-specific EPDs
Accuracy depends on BIM model material definitions
No operational energy or whole-life carbon calculation
citation: Building Transparency (2024). tallyCAT. https://www.buildingtransparency.org/tallycat/

name: IMPACT Specification
description: BRE Group's UK specification for whole-building LCA enabling compliant assessments in BIM-integrated tools using EN 15978 and EN 15804 methodology.
longDescription: IMPACT (Integrated Material Profile And Costing Tool) is a specification and methodology developed by BRE Group for conducting whole-building life-cycle assessments in the UK. Rather than a standalone tool, IMPACT is implemented by licensed software partners (One Click LCA, eTool, etc.) ensuring consistent EN 15978-compliant calculations. The specification defines how building materials are mapped to environmental data from BRE's LCIA database, which draws from EPD data and generic datasets. IMPACT compliance is required for BREEAM Mat 01 credits and is referenced in the GLA London Plan for whole-life carbon assessments.
author: BRE Group (UK)

license.name: Free specification (implemented by licensed software partners)

scope: Whole-building LCA specification covering all life-cycle modules (A–D) per EN 15978
limitations: Specification only — requires licensed third-party software to use
UK-focused environmental data and building typologies
Software implementation varies between licensed partners
BRE generic data may differ from manufacturer-specific EPDs
citation: BRE Group (2024). IMPACT. https://www.bregroup.com/impact/

name: Mesh Embodied Carbon Calculator
description: Free early-stage embodied carbon calculator for buildings, enabling quick estimates from floor area, building type, and structural system without detailed BIM models.
longDescription: The Mesh Embodied Carbon Calculator by Mesh Energy is a free web-based tool for estimating embodied carbon at early design stages when detailed material quantities are not yet available. Users input building type, gross floor area, number of storeys, and structural system to receive an embodied carbon estimate (kgCO2e/m²) with benchmarking against LETI targets and RIBA 2030 Climate Challenge thresholds. The tool uses benchmark data from published UK projects and industry databases to provide indicative A1–A5 and whole-life estimates, helping architects and clients set embodied carbon budgets during RIBA Stages 0–2.
author: Mesh Energy

license.name: Free embodied carbon calculator (registration for Meshwork community required)

scope: Early-stage embodied carbon estimation (kgCO2e/m²) from building parameters
limitations: Indicative estimates only — not for detailed LCA or compliance
UK benchmark data — may not apply to other construction markets
Limited structural system and building type options
No material-level customisation
citation: Mesh Energy (2024). Embodied Carbon Calculator. https://www.meshenergy.com

name: First Street Risk Factor
description: Freemium US property-level climate risk platform covering flood, fire, wind, heat, and air quality with 30-year forward-looking projections.
longDescription: First Street Foundation provides property-level climate risk scores for every property in the contiguous United States. The Risk Factor platform integrates peer-reviewed flood, wildfire, wind (hurricane), extreme heat, and air quality models to generate current and future risk scores (1–10) with 30-year projections under multiple climate scenarios (SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5). The flood model covers riverine, coastal, pluvial, and storm surge hazards at ~3m resolution. Risk Factor data is available via free property lookup (limited), API subscription, and is integrated into Realtor.com and Redfin listings for residential real estate.
author: First Street Foundation

license.name: Free basic property risk scores; paid for full reports, commercial data, and API

scope: Property-level flood, wildfire, wind, heat, and air quality risk scores with 30-year projections
limitations: US-only coverage (contiguous states)
Free tier limited to individual property lookups
Flood model resolution (~3m) may miss micro-topographic features
Risk scores are relative (1–10) — not direct financial loss estimates
citation: First Street Foundation (2024). Risk Factor. https://riskfactor.com

name: NOAA Sea Level Rise Viewer
description: Free interactive US coastal mapping tool showing inundation scenarios from 1–10 feet of sea level rise with flood frequency, marsh migration, and socioeconomic data layers.
longDescription: The NOAA Sea Level Rise Viewer is a free web-based mapping tool that visualises the potential impacts of sea level rise on US coastal areas. Users can simulate inundation from 1 to 10 feet of sea level rise over current mean higher high water (MHHW) and view affected areas, flood frequency changes, marsh migration, and socioeconomic impacts. The viewer uses high-resolution coastal elevation data (lidar-derived DEMs) and incorporates tidal surfaces, levee data, and hydrologic connectivity to produce realistic inundation extents. It covers the entire US coastline including Hawaii, US territories, and the Great Lakes.
author: NOAA Office for Coastal Management

license.name: Free public access (US government data, public domain)

scenario: 1–10 feet SLR scenarios (not tied to specific climate scenarios)
scope: Coastal inundation mapping, flood frequency, marsh migration, socioeconomic exposure
limitations: US coastline only
Bathtub model — does not simulate wave action or storm surge dynamics
Does not account for future land subsidence or uplift
Discrete foot increments (no sub-foot resolution)
No building-level damage or financial loss estimates
citation: NOAA (2024). Sea Level Rise Viewer. https://coast.noaa.gov/slr/

name: Climate Central Coastal Risk Screening
description: Global coastal flood and sea level rise screening tool with interactive maps showing land areas below projected flood levels under various warming scenarios.
longDescription: Climate Central's Coastal Risk Screening Tool provides global maps of areas vulnerable to coastal flooding and sea level rise. Using the CoastalDEM elevation model and peer-reviewed sea level projections, the tool shows land areas below projected flood levels for different warming scenarios (1.5°C to 4°C), time horizons (2030, 2050, 2100, 2150), and return periods (annual flood, 1-in-100-year flood). The free version provides map visualisation and area statistics; the premium Surging Seas Risk Finder (US only) adds property-level data and demographic analysis. Data covers all global coastlines with population and land area exposure statistics by country.
author: Climate Central (Princeton, NJ)

license.name: Free basic maps (non-commercial); commercial licensing for FloodVision and bulk data

scenario: 1.5°C to 4°C warming; multiple time horizons to 2150
scope: Global coastal flood exposure, sea level rise inundation, population and land area at risk
limitations: CoastalDEM has lower accuracy than lidar-based national DEMs
Bathtub inundation model — no hydrodynamic simulation
Property-level data limited to US (Surging Seas premium)
No building damage or financial loss estimates
citation: Climate Central (2024). Coastal Risk Screening Tool. https://coastal.climatecentral.org

name: ThinkHazard!
description: GFDRR/World Bank free multi-hazard screening tool providing hazard level classifications for any global location across 11 natural hazard types.
longDescription: ThinkHazard! is a free, open-source web tool developed by the Global Facility for Disaster Reduction and Recovery (GFDRR) and the World Bank. It provides hazard level classifications (high, medium, low, very low) for any global location across 11 natural hazard types: river flood, coastal flood, earthquake, landslide, tsunami, volcano, cyclone, extreme heat, wildfire, water scarcity, and urban flood. Hazard levels are derived from global hazard datasets (UNISDR, GFDRR, NASA, NOAA) and include climate change considerations. For each hazard, the tool provides contextual recommendations and links to detailed risk information.
author: GFDRR / World Bank
scenario: Current climate with climate change projections for selected hazards
scope: Multi-hazard screening (11 hazard types) with categorical risk levels and recommendations
limitations: Screening-level only — not suitable for detailed risk assessment
Administrative-area resolution (not property-level)
Categorical risk levels (high/medium/low) — no quantitative probability
Underlying datasets updated at varying frequencies
No financial loss or damage estimation
citation: GFDRR/World Bank (2024). ThinkHazard! https://thinkhazard.org

name: Wildfire Risk to Communities
description: USDA Forest Service free tool mapping wildfire risk, likelihood, and exposure for every community and census tract in the conterminous United States.
longDescription: Wildfire Risk to Communities is a free web tool developed by the USDA Forest Service that provides wildfire risk data for every community in the conterminous United States. The tool integrates wildfire likelihood (probability of a fire occurring), wildfire intensity (expected flame length), and exposure of homes and buildings to calculate a composite risk score. Data is available at census tract, county, and state levels. The underlying model uses the Large Fire Simulation System (FSim) with 10,000+ fire season simulations to estimate burn probability and flame length. Community-level risk profiles help identify which risk factors (likelihood vs. exposure vs. housing density) drive local risk.
author: USDA Forest Service

license.name: Free public access (US government data — USDA Forest Service)

scope: Wildfire likelihood, intensity, exposure, and composite risk by community and census tract
limitations: US conterminous states only (excludes Alaska and Hawaii)
Based on current/historical conditions — no future climate projections
Census-tract resolution — not property-level
Does not account for mitigation measures (defensible space, building materials)
No financial loss estimation
citation: USDA Forest Service (2024). Wildfire Risk to Communities. https://wildfirerisk.org

name: Zurich Natural Hazards Radar
description: Zurich Insurance's free property-level natural hazard check for Swiss addresses covering flood, storm, hail, earthquake, landslide, and surface water hazards.
longDescription: The Zurich Natural Hazards Radar (Naturgefahren-Radar) is a free online tool provided by Zurich Insurance Switzerland that assesses natural hazard exposure for any Swiss address. It evaluates flood, storm, hail, earthquake, landslide, rockfall, avalanche, and surface water (pluvial flooding) hazards using cantonal hazard maps and the Swiss natural hazards datasets from the Federal Office for the Environment (BAFU). The tool provides a hazard profile with risk ratings per hazard type and practical prevention recommendations. It is freely accessible to all users, not just Zurich Insurance customers.
author: Zurich Insurance Switzerland
scope: Property-level natural hazard screening for Swiss addresses (flood, storm, hail, earthquake, landslide, etc.)
limitations: Switzerland only
Screening-level — not a detailed engineering risk assessment
Based on cantonal hazard maps (varying update cycles)
No climate change projections — current hazard levels only
No financial loss or damage estimation
citation: Zurich Insurance Switzerland (2024). Natural Hazards Radar. https://www.zurich.ch

name: Fathom Global Flood Map
description: Commercial high-resolution global flood hazard model covering fluvial, pluvial, and coastal flooding with climate change scenarios, used by insurers and governments.
longDescription: Fathom is a flood risk analytics company (Bristol, UK) providing high-resolution global flood hazard maps and risk data. The Fathom Global model covers fluvial (river), pluvial (surface water), and coastal flood hazards at ~30m resolution for every country worldwide. The model simulates flood extents and depths for multiple return periods (5-year to 1000-year) under current and future climate scenarios (RCP 4.5/8.5, SSP2-4.5/SSP5-8.5). Fathom data is used by FEMA (US National Flood Hazard Layer validation), the World Bank, and major insurers for portfolio risk assessment. A free global flood map viewer is publicly available.
author: Fathom (Bristol, UK)

license.name: Commercial license; free non-commercial data for 16 vulnerable countries (via World Bank)

scope: Global flood hazard maps (fluvial, pluvial, coastal) — depths and extents by return period
limitations: Commercial product — full data requires paid license
30m resolution may miss small-scale urban features
Global model uses globally consistent but not locally calibrated hydrology
No building-level vulnerability or damage functions included
citation: Fathom (2024). Fathom Global Flood Map v3.0. https://www.fathom.global

name: XDI Climate Risk Hub
description: Asset-level physical climate risk analytics platform covering 8 hazards across all global locations with financial damage estimates under multiple climate scenarios.
longDescription: XDI (Cross Dependency Initiative), developed by the Climate Risk Group in Australia, provides asset-level physical climate risk analytics for buildings and infrastructure worldwide. The XDI Climate Risk Hub assesses 8 hazard types (riverine flood, coastal inundation, tropical cyclone, extreme wind, forest fire, soil movement, freeze-thaw, extreme heat) under multiple IPCC scenarios (SSP1-2.6 to SSP5-8.5) out to 2100. For each asset, XDI estimates annual average damage (AAL), technical insurance premium, and value-at-risk as a percentage of replacement cost. XDI's Gross Domestic Climate Risk ranking benchmarks climate risk exposure across 2,600+ global provinces.
author: XDI / Climate Risk Group (Australia)

license.name: Commercial SaaS; free Gross Domestic Climate Risk ranking

scope: Asset-level physical risk across 8 hazards — annual average loss, technical insurance premium, value-at-risk
limitations: Commercial product — portfolio-level access requires paid subscription
Global model may be less accurate than locally calibrated national models
Building vulnerability functions use generic construction archetypes
Financial damage estimates require assumed replacement cost inputs
citation: XDI / Climate Risk Group (2024). XDI Climate Risk Hub. https://xdi.systems

name: TNFD LEAP Framework
description: Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures integrated assessment approach (Locate, Evaluate, Assess, Prepare) for nature-related risk and opportunity disclosure.
longDescription: The TNFD (Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures) LEAP approach is an integrated assessment framework for organisations to identify and assess nature-related dependencies, impacts, risks, and opportunities. LEAP stands for: Locate (interface with nature), Evaluate (dependencies and impacts), Assess (material risks and opportunities), and Prepare (to respond and report). The framework aligns with the TCFD structure (governance, strategy, risk management, metrics and targets) and is designed to work alongside existing ESG and climate disclosure frameworks. TNFD v1.0 recommendations were published in September 2023 and are being adopted by financial regulators and investors globally.
author: Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD)
scope: Nature-related dependencies, impacts, risks, and opportunities assessment and disclosure
limitations: Framework/guidance only — no calculation engine or tool
Requires complementary data tools (ENCORE, IBAT) for implementation
Nature-related metrics still evolving — less standardised than climate metrics
Sector-specific guidance still under development for some industries
citation: TNFD (2023). Recommendations of the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures v1.0. https://tnfd.global

name: Defra Statutory Biodiversity Metric
description: England's mandatory Biodiversity Net Gain calculator measuring habitat value in biodiversity units before and after development to demonstrate 10% net gain.
longDescription: The Defra Statutory Biodiversity Metric (v4.0) is the mandatory calculation tool for Biodiversity Net Gain (BNG) in England under the Environment Act 2021. All major developments must demonstrate a minimum 10% biodiversity net gain by calculating habitat biodiversity units pre- and post-development. The metric scores habitats based on distinctiveness, condition, extent (area or length), strategic significance, and connectivity. It covers area habitats, hedgerow habitats, and watercourse habitats. The tool is an Excel spreadsheet with automated calculations and outputs a BNG statement suitable for planning application submission.
author: Defra / Natural England

license.name: Free download (UK government — statutory biodiversity metric Excel tool)

scope: Biodiversity unit calculation for area habitats, hedgerows, and watercourses — pre- and post-development
limitations: England only (separate approaches in Wales, Scotland, NI)
Excel-based — no web or GIS integration
Requires ecological survey data (competent ecologist needed)
Does not measure species-level biodiversity — habitat proxy only
30-year habitat management obligation difficult to enforce
citation: Defra / Natural England (2024). Statutory Biodiversity Metric v4.0. https://www.gov.uk

name: ENCORE
description: Free tool mapping dependencies and impacts of economic sectors on natural capital, enabling nature-related risk screening for financial portfolios.
longDescription: ENCORE (Exploring Natural Capital Opportunities, Risks and Exposure) is a free web-based tool developed by UNEP-WCMC and Global Canopy (funded by the Swiss State Secretariat for Economic Affairs, MAVA Foundation, and others). It maps the dependencies and impacts of 157 economic sub-sectors on 21 ecosystem services and natural capital assets. For each sector-nature linkage, ENCORE provides materiality ratings (very high to low) and qualitative descriptions. The tool enables financial institutions to screen portfolios for nature-related dependencies and transition risks at sector level, supporting TNFD LEAP assessments and natural capital risk management.
author: UNEP-WCMC / Global Canopy
scope: Sector-level dependencies and impacts on 21 ecosystem services and natural capital assets
adoptedBy: Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS)
limitations: Sector-level analysis only — not asset or location-specific
Qualitative materiality ratings — no quantitative financial impact
Based on expert assessment — limited empirical validation
157 sub-sectors may not capture all real estate-specific nuances
citation: UNEP-WCMC / Global Canopy (2024). ENCORE. https://encore.naturalcapital.finance

name: IBAT Biodiversity Screening
description: Freemium tool providing proximity screening against IUCN Red List species, protected areas (WDPA), and Key Biodiversity Areas for project sites and portfolios.
longDescription: IBAT (Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool) is a biodiversity screening platform developed by BirdLife International, Conservation International, IUCN, and UNEP-WCMC. It provides proximity analysis for any global location against three authoritative biodiversity datasets: the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species, the World Database on Protected Areas (WDPA), and the World Database of Key Biodiversity Areas (KBA). Users can generate proximity reports, multi-site screening reports, and country profiles. IBAT is used by financial institutions, extractive industries, and infrastructure developers for biodiversity risk screening and due diligence under IFC Performance Standard 6 and TNFD.
author: BirdLife International / Conservation International / IUCN / UNEP-WCMC

license.name: Free biodiversity maps; subscription for reports and data downloads

scope: Proximity analysis to IUCN Red List species, protected areas (WDPA), and Key Biodiversity Areas
limitations: Screening-level proximity analysis — not a detailed biodiversity impact assessment
Multi-site and portfolio features require paid subscription
Species data resolution varies by region and taxonomic group
Does not assess actual biodiversity impacts — proximity only
citation: IBAT Alliance (2024). IBAT. https://www.ibat-alliance.org

name: WWF Biodiversity Risk Filter
description: Free portfolio-level biodiversity risk screening tool assessing physical and reputational risks from biodiversity loss across company operations and supply chains.
longDescription: The WWF Biodiversity Risk Filter is a free online tool that enables companies and financial institutions to screen their operations, supply chains, and investment portfolios for biodiversity-related risks. It assesses both physical risks (dependency on ecosystem services, proximity to biodiversity-sensitive areas) and reputational risks (media attention, regulatory pressure) at a location and sector level. The tool combines spatial biodiversity data (IUCN, WDPA, KBA, intact forest landscapes) with sector-specific dependency scores to generate risk heat maps and priority location lists. Version 5.0 aligns with TNFD and Science Based Targets for Nature (SBTN).
author: WWF (World Wide Fund for Nature)

license.name: Free (registration optional; required for Assess module only)

scope: Portfolio and location-level biodiversity physical and reputational risk screening
limitations: Screening-level — not a detailed ecological assessment
Sector-level dependency scores may not capture site-specific conditions
Reputational risk scoring is qualitative
No financial quantification of biodiversity risks
citation: WWF (2024). Biodiversity Risk Filter v5.0. https://riskfilter.org/biodiversity

name: WWF Water Risk Filter
description: Free portfolio-level water risk screening tool assessing physical, regulatory, and reputational water risks for company operations and investments worldwide.
longDescription: The WWF Water Risk Filter is a free online tool that enables companies and financial institutions to assess water-related risks across their operations, supply chains, and investment portfolios. It evaluates physical risks (water scarcity, flooding, water quality, ecosystem status), regulatory risks (water governance, water-related policies), and reputational risks (media attention, community conflicts) at basin and sub-basin level globally. The tool uses data from WRI Aqueduct, WHO/UNICEF JMP, and WWF's own hydrological models. Version 6.0 includes climate change projections (SSP2 and SSP3 scenarios to 2030 and 2050) and aligns with CEO Water Mandate, CDP Water, and TNFD.
author: WWF (World Wide Fund for Nature) / DEG

license.name: Free (registration optional; required for Assess module only)

scope: Portfolio and location-level water physical, regulatory, and reputational risk screening
limitations: Basin-level resolution — not property or site-specific
Regulatory and reputational risk scores are qualitative
Water quality data coverage varies by region
No financial quantification of water risks
citation: WWF (2024). Water Risk Filter v6.0. https://riskfilter.org/water

name: EU Taxonomy Navigator
description: European Commission's free tools for navigating EU Taxonomy technical screening criteria and calculating taxonomy alignment for sustainable economic activities.
longDescription: The EU Taxonomy Compass and Calculator are free web tools provided by the European Commission to help companies, financial institutions, and investors navigate and apply the EU Taxonomy for Sustainable Activities (Regulation 2020/852). The Compass provides a searchable database of all economic activities and their Technical Screening Criteria (TSC) for six environmental objectives (climate mitigation, adaptation, water, circular economy, pollution, biodiversity). The Calculator helps assess taxonomy alignment by checking substantial contribution criteria and Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) conditions. For real estate, key activities include construction of new buildings (activity 7.1), renovation (7.2), and acquisition/ownership (7.7).
author: European Commission
scope: EU Taxonomy technical screening criteria navigation and alignment assessment for all six environmental objectives
limitations: EU Taxonomy criteria only — not applicable outside EU regulatory scope
Compass is navigational — does not perform detailed compliance calculations
TSC interpretation can be subjective for some criteria
Delegated acts still evolving — criteria subject to change
citation: European Commission (2024). EU Taxonomy Compass. https://ec.europa.eu/sustainable-finance-taxonomy/

name: TPI Online Tool
description: Free tool assessing corporate transition preparedness by scoring management quality and carbon performance alignment against Paris-aligned benchmarks for listed companies.
longDescription: The Transition Pathway Initiative (TPI) Online Tool is a free web-based tool developed by the Grantham Research Institute at LSE and FTSE Russell. It assesses the climate transition preparedness of listed companies across high-emitting sectors (including real estate, construction materials, and utilities). TPI evaluates two dimensions: Management Quality (governance, targets, disclosure scored 0–4) and Carbon Performance (alignment of company emissions trajectories against Paris-aligned sectoral benchmarks to 2050). The tool covers 900+ companies and is used by 140+ investors managing $50+ trillion in assets for engagement and investment decisions.
author: Grantham Research Institute (LSE) / LSEG (formerly FTSE Russell)

license.name: Free public access (open-access data and methodology)

scenario: IEA NZE 2050, IEA B2DS, NDC benchmarks
scope: Corporate management quality scoring and carbon performance alignment against Paris-aligned sectoral pathways
limitations: Listed companies only — no private company or asset-level coverage
Sector benchmarks may not capture company-specific decarbonization contexts
Management quality scoring based on public disclosure only
Real estate sector coverage limited to largest listed REITs
citation: TPI (2024). TPI Online Tool. https://www.transitionpathwayinitiative.org

name: Arc EU Taxonomy Self-Assessment Tool
description: Free tool by GBCI mapping LEED credit achievements to EU Taxonomy technical screening criteria for demonstrating taxonomy alignment of certified green buildings.
longDescription: The Arc platform's EU Taxonomy Alignment Tool, developed by Green Business Certification Inc. (GBCI) and USGBC, maps LEED certification credits and performance data to EU Taxonomy Technical Screening Criteria (TSC) for real estate activities. The tool helps building owners and investors demonstrate EU Taxonomy alignment for LEED-certified buildings by cross-referencing achieved LEED credits with TSC requirements for climate change mitigation (Activity 7.1–7.7) and Do No Significant Harm conditions. This enables reporting under SFDR, CSRD, and the EU Green Bond Standard without conducting separate taxonomy assessments from scratch.
author: GBCI / USGBC

license.name: Free (Arc platform registration required; LEED certification fees separate)

scope: LEED credit-to-EU Taxonomy TSC mapping for real estate taxonomy alignment reporting
limitations: Only applicable to LEED-certified buildings
Mapping may not cover all TSC requirements — supplementary evidence may be needed
EU Taxonomy TSC continue to evolve — mapping requires updates
Limited to climate mitigation objective — other objectives not fully covered
citation: GBCI (2024). Arc EU Taxonomy Alignment Tool. https://arcskoru.com

name: Evercity Taxonomy Screening
description: Freemium digital platform for EU Taxonomy eligibility and alignment screening, green bond framework development, and sustainable finance reporting.
longDescription: Evercity is a digital platform for sustainable finance that provides EU Taxonomy eligibility and alignment screening tools for companies, financial institutions, and green bond issuers. The taxonomy screening module guides users through Technical Screening Criteria (TSC) for each economic activity, checks Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) conditions, and verifies minimum social safeguards. The platform also supports green bond framework development, impact reporting, and verification workflows. Evercity's free tier provides basic taxonomy screening, while premium features include portfolio-level analysis, automated data collection, and verifier collaboration.
author: Evercity
scope: EU Taxonomy eligibility/alignment screening, green bond frameworks, impact reporting
limitations: Advanced features require paid subscription
EU Taxonomy focus — limited support for other regional taxonomies
TSC interpretation guidance is platform-specific
Verification features depend on third-party verifier availability
citation: Evercity (2024). Evercity Platform. https://evercity.io

name: PACTA Global
description: RMI's open-source Paris Agreement Capital Transition Assessment tool for measuring financial portfolio alignment with climate scenarios across high-emitting sectors.
longDescription: PACTA (Paris Agreement Capital Transition Assessment) is an open-source methodology and tool developed by RMI (formerly 2° Investing Initiative/2DII) for assessing the alignment of financial portfolios with climate transition scenarios. PACTA maps financial holdings to company-level production and capacity data across climate-relevant sectors (power, fossil fuels, automotive, steel, cement, aviation) and compares forward-looking production plans against IEA climate scenarios (NZE 2050, APS, STEPS). The tool produces sector-level alignment metrics, technology mix exposures, and aggregate portfolio temperature scores. PACTA is available as a free online tool, R package, and API.
author: RMI (Rocky Mountain Institute)
scenario: IEA NZE 2050, APS, STEPS; NGFS scenarios
scope: Financial portfolio alignment with climate transition scenarios across high-emitting sectors
limitations: Sector coverage limited to high-emitting industries — real estate not directly covered
Company-level data dependent on asset-level databases (e.g., Asset Resolution)
Forward-looking production plans may not materialise
Temperature scores involve significant methodological uncertainty
citation: RMI (2024). PACTA. https://pacta.rmi.org

name: GHG Protocol Calculation Tools
description: WRI/WBCSD's free suite of Excel-based tools and emission factor databases for corporate GHG accounting across Scope 1, 2, and 3 under the GHG Protocol standards.
longDescription: The GHG Protocol Calculation Tools are a suite of free Excel-based calculators and emission factor databases developed by the World Resources Institute (WRI) and the World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD). The tools support corporate GHG accounting under the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard and Scope 3 Standard. Key tools include: cross-sector tools for stationary combustion, purchased electricity, mobile combustion, and refrigeration/AC; sector-specific tools for cement, aluminium, iron/steel, etc.; and the Scope 3 Evaluator for screening all 15 Scope 3 categories. Emission factors are provided for 100+ countries with regular updates.
author: WRI (World Resources Institute) / WBCSD
scope: Corporate GHG emissions calculation — Scope 1, 2, and 3 across all source categories
limitations: Excel-based tools — no web application or API
Emission factors may lag current grid decarbonization
Tools are calculators, not an integrated carbon management platform
Building-specific tools limited — use with sector-specific guidance
citation: WRI/WBCSD (2024). GHG Protocol Calculation Tools. https://ghgprotocol.org

name: RICS Whole Life Carbon Assessment Standard
description: RICS professional standard providing a harmonised methodology for whole-life carbon assessment of buildings covering embodied, operational, and end-of-life carbon.
longDescription: The RICS Whole Life Carbon Assessment (WLCA) Standard (2nd edition, 2023) provides a harmonised methodology for measuring the total carbon impact of buildings across their entire life cycle. It covers all life-cycle modules per EN 15978: embodied carbon (Modules A1–A5, B1–B5, C1–C4), operational carbon (Module B6 — regulated and unregulated energy, B7 — water), and benefits beyond the system boundary (Module D). The standard specifies mandatory reporting modules, benchmark comparison requirements, data quality expectations, and a standardised reporting template. It is mandatory for BREEAM assessments and referenced by the GLA London Plan, LETI, and the UK Net Zero Carbon Buildings Standard.
author: Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (RICS)
scope: Whole-life carbon assessment methodology — Modules A1–A5, B1–B7, C1–C4, D per EN 15978
limitations: Assessment standard/methodology — not a calculation tool
Requires LCA software and EPD data for implementation
UK/European origin — some requirements may not align with other regional standards
2nd edition transition period — some assessors still using 1st edition
citation: RICS (2023). Whole life carbon assessment for the built environment, 2nd edition. https://www.rics.org

name: UK Net Zero Carbon Buildings Standard
description: Cross-industry UK standard defining net zero carbon for buildings with performance limits for operational energy, embodied carbon, and offsetting requirements.
longDescription: The UK Net Zero Carbon Buildings Standard (NZCBS) is a cross-industry initiative led by CIBSE, BRE, RIBA, RICS, LETI, UKGBC, and the Institution of Structural Engineers, with technical development by Arup and Sturgis Carbon Profiling. The standard defines what it means for a building to be net zero carbon across two frameworks: Net Zero Carbon — Construction (embodied carbon limits for Modules A1–A5 and whole-life A–C) and Net Zero Carbon — Operation (in-use energy performance limits with space heating, DHW, lighting, and unregulated loads). Buildings must meet absolute performance limits before any offsetting. The pilot version launched in 2024 with full launch planned for 2025.
author: CIBSE / BRE / RIBA / RICS / LETI / UKGBC / IStructE
scope: Net zero carbon verification — operational energy limits and embodied carbon limits with offsetting framework
limitations: UK-focused — climate and energy context specific to UK
Pilot phase — standard may evolve before full launch
Verification methodology still being refined
Limited building type coverage in pilot (offices, residential, schools)
citation: NZCBS Partnership (2024). UK Net Zero Carbon Buildings Standard — Pilot. https://www.nzcbuildings.co.uk

name: UKGBC Net Zero Carbon Buildings Framework
description: UKGBC's framework definition and guidance for achieving net zero carbon in construction and operation of buildings, with energy hierarchy and offsetting principles. Note: Being superseded by the UK Net Zero Carbon Buildings Standard (UK NZCBS).
longDescription: The UKGBC Net Zero Carbon Buildings Framework Definition provides guidance on what constitutes a net zero carbon building in the UK context. Published in 2019, it defines two scopes: Net Zero Carbon — Construction (reducing and offsetting embodied emissions from Modules A1–A5) and Net Zero Carbon — Operational Energy (reducing operational energy through efficiency and renewables, then offsetting residual emissions). The framework establishes an energy hierarchy (reduce demand, use renewables, offset residual) and sets principles for credible offsetting. It has been widely adopted as the basis for net zero carbon commitments by UK developers, local authorities, and the subsequent UK NZCBS development.
author: UK Green Building Council (UKGBC)
scope: Net zero carbon definition, energy hierarchy, and offsetting principles for buildings
limitations: Framework definition — not a calculation tool or standard
2019 publication — being superseded by UK NZCBS for verification
Offsetting principles less stringent than subsequent standards
No specific numeric performance targets (those provided by LETI, RIBA, NZCBS)
citation: UKGBC (2019). Net Zero Carbon Buildings: A Framework Definition. https://ukgbc.org

name: BBP Real Estate Environmental Benchmark
description: UK's largest operational sustainability benchmark for commercial real estate, providing energy, water, and waste performance comparisons across property types.
longDescription: The Better Buildings Partnership (BBP) Real Estate Environmental Benchmark (REEB) is the UK's largest operational sustainability benchmark for commercial real estate. It collects annual energy consumption, carbon emissions, water use, and waste data from participating landlords and provides anonymised performance benchmarks by property type (office, retail, industrial, residential). REEB reports median, upper quartile, and lower quartile performance metrics (kWh/m²/yr, kgCO2e/m²/yr, litres/m²/yr) enabling building owners to benchmark their portfolios against industry peers. REEB data informs CRREM pathway calibration, NABERS UK development, and MEES policy discussions.
author: Better Buildings Partnership (BBP)
scope: Operational energy, carbon, water, and waste benchmarks for UK commercial real estate by property type
limitations: UK commercial real estate only
Benchmark data from BBP member portfolios — may not represent full market
Annual reporting — no real-time or monthly data
Residential coverage limited
Participation is voluntary — self-reported data quality varies
citation: BBP (2024). Real Estate Environmental Benchmark (REEB). https://www.betterbuildingspartnership.co.uk

name: CARE Tool
description: Architecture 2030's free Carbon Avoided Retrofit Estimator for comparing embodied carbon of renovation versus new construction to support retrofit-first decision-making.
longDescription: The CARE (Carbon Avoided: Retrofit Estimator) Tool is a free web-based calculator developed by Architecture 2030 that helps architects, building owners, and policymakers quantify the carbon benefit of retrofitting an existing building versus demolishing and rebuilding. Users input building type, floor area, structural system, and renovation scope to compare the embodied carbon of a deep retrofit against new construction. The tool uses data from the CLF Embodied Carbon Benchmark Database and LETI benchmarks to estimate carbon avoided through reuse of existing structure. CARE supports the business case for adaptive reuse and retrofit-first policies.
author: Architecture 2030
scope: Embodied carbon comparison between retrofit and new construction — carbon avoided through building reuse
limitations: Simplified estimation — not a detailed LCA
US benchmark data — may not directly apply to other construction markets
Does not account for operational energy improvements from new construction
Limited structural system and building type options
citation: Architecture 2030 (2024). CARE Tool. https://caretool.org

name: GEAK Building Energy Certificate
description: Swiss cantonal building energy certificate (Gebäudeenergieausweis der Kantone) rating buildings A–G for envelope and overall energy efficiency, with renovation advisory reports.
longDescription: GEAK (Gebäudeenergieausweis der Kantone) is the official Swiss cantonal building energy certificate system. It rates buildings on two scales (A to G): envelope efficiency (thermal quality of building shell) and overall energy efficiency (including heating system and renewables). GEAK assessments are performed by certified GEAK experts using standardised calculation tools based on SIA 380/1 heating energy demand methodology. The GEAK Plus report extends the basic certificate with a detailed renovation advisory including up to three renovation scenarios with cost estimates, energy savings, and CO2 reduction. GEAK is mandatory for some cantonal subsidy programs and real estate transactions.
author: GEAK Association / Conference of Cantonal Energy Directors (EnDK)

license.name: Restricted to certified GEAK experts (cantonal energy certificate system)

scope: Building energy rating (A–G) for envelope and overall efficiency, renovation advisory scenarios
limitations: Switzerland only — cantonal system
Assessment restricted to certified GEAK experts (not self-service)
Paid assessment (CHF 500–2000 depending on building size and GEAK Plus)
Calculation methodology based on SIA 380/1 — standardised conditions, not actual consumption
Energy label does not directly indicate carbon performance
citation: GEAK Association (2024). GEAK. https://www.geak.ch

name: Sonnenfassade.ch
description: Free Swiss tool by SFOE and swisstopo estimating solar electricity and heat generation potential on building facades across Switzerland.
longDescription: Sonnenfassade.ch is a free web-based tool developed by the Swiss Federal Office of Energy (SFOE) and swisstopo that estimates the solar energy potential of building facades across Switzerland. Complementing the rooftop-focused Sonnendach.ch, this tool calculates the annual solar irradiation on every facade surface of Swiss buildings using 3D building models and terrain data. Users can assess the potential for facade-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV) and solar thermal collectors. The tool displays facade-level solar potential maps and provides estimates of annual electricity (kWh) and heat production for each building, supporting Switzerland's Energy Strategy 2050 goals.
author: Swiss Federal Office of Energy (SFOE) / swisstopo

license.name: Free public tool (Swiss Federal Office of Energy); data open on opendata.swiss

scope: Building facade solar irradiation and PV/thermal generation potential for all Swiss buildings
limitations: Switzerland only
Facade solar potential only — for rooftops use Sonnendach.ch
3D model resolution may miss architectural details (balconies, overhangs)
Does not account for shading from vegetation or future development
Estimated potential — actual yield depends on PV system design and orientation
citation: SFOE / swisstopo (2024). Sonnenfassade.ch. https://www.sonnenfassade.ch

name: MINERGIE Certification Tools
description: Swiss building energy standard and certification system (Minergie, Minergie-P, Minergie-A, Minergie-ECO) with online calculation and application tools.
longDescription: MINERGIE is Switzerland's leading voluntary building energy standard, operated by the Minergie Association. It offers four certification levels: Minergie (efficient new builds and renovations), Minergie-P (near-Passive House standard), Minergie-A (net-zero energy), and the ECO add-on (healthy and ecological construction including embodied energy and indoor air quality). The Minergie online tools include the application form with integrated energy calculation, the MKG cost tool, and the Minergie module database for certified building components. Calculation methodology is based on SIA 380/1 and SIA 380 standards. Over 55,000 buildings are Minergie-certified in Switzerland.
author: Minergie Association (Switzerland)

license.name: Free application tools and regulations; certification fee applies

scope: Building energy performance certification — heating demand, primary energy, airtightness, comfort ventilation
limitations: Switzerland-specific standard (not internationally recognised like Passive House)
Certification fee and application effort required
ECO add-on covers embodied energy but not full LCA
Standard conditions — certified performance may differ from actual consumption
citation: Minergie Association (2024). MINERGIE. https://www.minergie.ch

name: Das Gebäudeprogramm Calculator
description: Swiss federal/cantonal building program subsidy calculator estimating available grants for energy-efficient renovations, heating replacements, and envelope improvements.
longDescription: Das Gebäudeprogramm (The Buildings Programme) is a joint initiative of the Swiss Confederation and cantons that provides financial subsidies for energy-efficient building renovations. The online calculator allows building owners to estimate available subsidies by entering their canton, building type, and planned renovation measures (envelope insulation, window replacement, heating system replacement, solar installations, etc.). Subsidy amounts vary by canton and measure type, typically ranging from CHF 10–100/m² for envelope measures and CHF 2,000–10,000 for heating system replacements. The program is funded by the CO2 levy on fossil fuels and cantonal energy funds.
author: Swiss Confederation / Conference of Cantonal Energy Directors (EnDK)

license.name: Free public tool (Swiss cantonal subsidy information portal)

scope: Estimated cantonal subsidies for building envelope, heating system, and renewable energy renovation measures
limitations: Switzerland only — cantonal subsidy programs
Estimates only — actual subsidy amounts confirmed after application
Subsidy budgets may be exhausted in some cantons
Does not calculate energy savings or CO2 reduction — subsidy amounts only
Cantonal programs change annually — calculator may lag updates
citation: Das Gebäudeprogramm (2024). Subsidy Calculator. https://www.dasgebaeudeprogramm.ch

name: SIA 380/1 Calculators
description: Swiss SIA standard 380/1 heating energy demand calculators used for cantonal energy code compliance, GEAK assessments, and Minergie certification applications.
longDescription: SIA 380/1 (Heizwärmebedarf / Besoin de chaleur pour le chauffage) is the foundational Swiss engineering standard for calculating heating energy demand of buildings. Published by the Swiss Society of Engineers and Architects (SIA), it defines the calculation methodology for transmission and ventilation heat losses, solar and internal heat gains, and resulting heating energy demand (Qh) in kWh/m²/yr. Compliance with SIA 380/1 limit values is mandatory under the MuKEn (Mustervorschriften der Kantone im Energiebereich) model cantonal energy regulations. Several software implementations exist (Lesosai, Flixo, Polysun, cantonal tools), all based on the SIA 380/1 methodology. The standard also underpins GEAK energy certificates and Minergie certification calculations.
author: SIA (Swiss Society of Engineers and Architects) / cantonal energy offices

license.name: SIA standard (paid purchase at shop.sia.ch); calculator implementations vary

scope: Heating energy demand calculation (Qh in kWh/m²/yr) for Swiss building code compliance
limitations: Switzerland-specific standard and climate data
Heating demand only — does not cover cooling, lighting, or DHW (see SIA 380/4, 385/2)
Standardised occupancy and climate conditions — actual consumption may differ
SIA standard document requires purchase; software tools have varying costs
Multiple software implementations may produce slightly different results
citation: SIA (2024). SIA 380/1 Heizwärmebedarf. https://www.sia.ch
